name: Beneath the Bramblewood
notes:
Willowmere is a small, quiet town at the edge of a forest that has forgotten how to be alive. The birds don't sing anymore. The children keep bringing home strange glowing mushrooms from the treeline. The woman who runs the tavern hasn't slept in two weeks. Nobody knows why, but everyone feels it — something is wrong in the Bramblewood, and whatever it is, it's getting closer.
Beneath the roots of the oldest tree in the forest, a spirit named Mira has been sleeping for a very long time. She didn't mean to. She was the heart of the Bramblewood — the reason the moss grew soft and the fireflies came out in summer and the foxes knew which paths were safe. When she fell into her cursed sleep, the forest didn't die. It just slowly started forgetting what it was supposed to be.

areas.name: Willowmere
areas.notes: A drowsy little town at the edge of the Bramblewood forest, Willowmere is the kind of place where everyone knows everyone, the baker leaves muffins on your doorstep when you're sick, and the worst thing that usually happens is old Mr. Thistlewick's goat escaping again. Cobblestone paths wind between flower-box cottages, a small market square sits at the center with a splashing fountain, and paper lanterns hang between buildings that glow warm orange at night. Lately though, the forest has gone quiet — no birdsong, no rustling — and some of the children have started finding strange glowing mushrooms at the treeline.

areas.name: The Bramblewood
areas.notes:
The Bramblewood starts friendly enough — tall oak trees, soft moss underfoot, shafts of golden light filtering through the canopy. But about ten minutes in, the light gets dimmer, the trees grow closer together, and the path starts to feel less like a path and more like a suggestion. The strange glowing mushrooms from the town's treeline are everywhere here, forming rings and clusters that pulse faintly blue-green in the dark. They don't hurt anyone. They're just watching. (They're not actually watching. Probably.)
The forest has three distinct zones:

🌤️ The Sunny Edge
The part Pip knows. Fireflies even during the day, a small creek with smooth stones, a hollow log big enough to crawl through. This is where the party finds their first clue — a child's wooden toy, half-buried, that doesn't belong to anyone in town. A good place for a small easy encounter to warm up a 6-year-old player.
Encounter: The Tangled Fox
A fox with its tail caught in enchanted brambles, panicking. Freeing it is a simple skill challenge — maybe a DEX check to carefully work the thorns loose, or just good roleplay. If freed, the fox follows the party for a while and leads them away from one danger later. No stats needed. Just a good fox.

🍄 The Mushroom Maze
Deeper in. The glowing mushrooms here grow tall as the players — some taller. The path splits constantly and looks identical in every direction. Navigation feels impossible.
The trick: the mushrooms pulse in a slow rhythm, and if you hum or sing, the ones pointing toward the center of the forest glow brighter. Old Wren would know this. Pip would accidentally discover it. A good moment for the mom to let her daughter just start singing and then confirm — yes, it's working, the mushrooms like your voice.
Encounter: The Spore Sprites
Three tiny fae creatures, mischievous but not evil, who have been redirecting travelers away from the center of the forest because they're also scared of what's down there. They speak in rhymes, steal small objects, and can be won over with food (Marta's muffins work perfectly if the party thought to bring any). Once befriended they give the party a glowing seed — important later.
Stats: use Pixie stat block, CR 1/4, but make them giggly and non-threatening. They run if combat starts.

🌑 The Dark Heart
The center of the Bramblewood. The trees here are ancient and enormous, roots erupting from the ground like frozen waves. No mushrooms here — that should feel wrong after the last zone. No sound at all. In the middle is a massive old oak, twice the size of anything else, with a crack running down its trunk that glows faint red from somewhere far below.
This is the entrance to the catacombs.
At the base of the tree, the party finds evidence someone has been here recently — a camp, cold fire, the same boot prints from The Stranger's mud-caked boots. And scratched into the bark at eye level, a single sentence:
"Don't wake her until you're ready."
Encounter: The Bramble Guardian
A large treant-like creature made of twisted branches and old roots that blocks the crack in the tree. It's not a villain — it's been standing guard for a very long time, keeping something in. It will only let the party pass if they can prove they are brave and kind, not just strong.
Suggested challenge: it asks three questions. Something like — "Have you helped someone today? Have you been afraid and done it anyway? Do you promise to be careful down there?" If the party answers honestly, it steps aside. If they try to fight it, it's CR 5 and will absolutely win, but it doesn't attack first and always gives them another chance to just answer the questions.
Once it steps aside, it hands the party a single braided root bracelet and says: "She made me. I miss her. Bring her back if you can."

areas.name: The Catacombs
areas.notes:
The crack in the ancient oak opens into a narrow stone staircase, cool and smell­ing of earth and old rain. The walls are carved — not rough like a cave, but intentional, covered in flowing patterns of vines and stars and small animals. Someone built this a long time ago, and they built it beautifully. Torches in iron brackets still burn with a soft blue flame that never goes out. Whatever is down here has been expecting visitors.The catacombs have three chambers before the final room.
 Chamber One: The Hall of EchoesA long corridor lined with small alcoves, each containing a stone animal — a fox, a deer, a bird, an owl. As the party walks through, the animals' eyes follow them. Not threatening. Curious.If the party stops and talks to one — just says hello, introduces themselves — it animates briefly, nods, and the blue torches burn a little brighter. If the party rushes through ignoring them, a low rumble fills the hall and the torches dim.This chamber is purely about tone-setting and teaching the 6-year-old that kindness is the mechanic down here, not combat.Snip and Fuzz are here. Cowering in the last alcove, pretending to be stone statues, very badly. They got lost following the party into the forest and ended up down here by accident. They are absolutely terrified and will attach themselves to the group immediately. Fuzz finds a spoon on the floor. It is an ordinary spoon. He keeps it.🌊 Chamber Two: The Flooded RoomA wide chamber, knee-deep in perfectly still black water. Stepping in reveals the water is full of stars — not reflection, actual tiny lights drifting slowly like a night sky underfoot. Beautiful and a little eerie.Halfway across, something is wrong. The floor drops suddenly in the center — too deep to walk across. The party needs to find another way.The glowing seed from the Sprites is used here. If planted in a crack in the wall beside the water, it grows instantly into a bridge of thick roots and vines, arching across the deep section. The seed is gone after this. That's okay. That's what it was for.Snip tries to take a shortcut and falls in the deep part. Fuzz pulls him out using the spoon as a reaching tool, which should not work but does. This is their heroic moment.Encounter: The Star Jellyfish
Three large bioluminescent jellyfish drifting in the water. They are not aggressive but will bump into anyone moving too fast, dealing 1d4 lightning damage. Moving slowly and calmly through the water, they part. A good lesson in patience. The party can name them if they want. Suggested names already on hand: Squeaky, Umbra, Noctis, Pebble, Thistle. Use whichever didn't get used for bats.🧩 Chamber Three: The Puzzle RoomA circular room with a locked stone door and a mosaic floor depicting the Bramblewood — the town, the forest, the mushrooms, the guardian, all of it. Around the edge of the room are five stone pedestals, each with a symbol: a flame, a leaf, a drop of water, a paw print, and a heart.The root bracelet from the Guardian glows when held near the heart pedestal. Placing it there causes that symbol to light up. The party needs to figure out the other four:
Flame — light a torch and hold it near (or a fire cantrip)
Leaf — place any natural item from the forest (the fox's fur works, or a leaf from the Sunny Edge if they grabbed one)
Water — pour water from the flooded room (if they thought to bring any) or a water cantrip
Paw print — the fox steps on it on its own, without being asked, right when the party is stuck
That last one should get a reaction. The fox knew.The door opens with a sound like wind through branches and the smell of something green and alive rushing out.

The Catacombs Beneath the Bramblewood
The crack in the ancient oak opens into a narrow stone staircase, cool and smell­ing of earth and old rain. The walls are carved — not rough like a cave, but intentional, covered in flowing patterns of vines and stars and small animals. Someone built this a long time ago, and they built it beautifully. Torches in iron brackets still burn with a soft blue flame that never goes out. Whatever is down here has been expecting visitors.
The catacombs have three chambers before the final room.

🕯️ Chamber One: The Hall of Echoes
A long corridor lined with small alcoves, each containing a stone animal — a fox, a deer, a bird, an owl. As the party walks through, the animals' eyes follow them. Not threatening. Curious.
If the party stops and talks to one — just says hello, introduces themselves — it animates briefly, nods, and the blue torches burn a little brighter. If the party rushes through ignoring them, a low rumble fills the hall and the torches dim.
This chamber is purely about tone-setting and teaching the 6-year-old that kindness is the mechanic down here, not combat.
Snip and Fuzz are here. Cowering in the last alcove, pretending to be stone statues, very badly. They got lost following the party into the forest and ended up down here by accident. They are absolutely terrified and will attach themselves to the group immediately. Fuzz finds a spoon on the floor. It is an ordinary spoon. He keeps it.

🌊 Chamber Two: The Flooded Room
A wide chamber, knee-deep in perfectly still black water. Stepping in reveals the water is full of stars — not reflection, actual tiny lights drifting slowly like a night sky underfoot. Beautiful and a little eerie.
Halfway across, something is wrong. The floor drops suddenly in the center — too deep to walk across. The party needs to find another way.
The glowing seed from the Sprites is used here. If planted in a crack in the wall beside the water, it grows instantly into a bridge of thick roots and vines, arching across the deep section. The seed is gone after this. That's okay. That's what it was for.
Snip tries to take a shortcut and falls in the deep part. Fuzz pulls him out using the spoon as a reaching tool, which should not work but does. This is their heroic moment.
Encounter: The Star Jellyfish
Three large bioluminescent jellyfish drifting in the water. They are not aggressive but will bump into anyone moving too fast, dealing 1d4 lightning damage. Moving slowly and calmly through the water, they part. A good lesson in patience. The party can name them if they want. Suggested names already on hand: Squeaky, Umbra, Noctis, Pebble, Thistle. Use whichever didn't get used for bats.

🧩 Chamber Three: The Puzzle Room
A circular room with a locked stone door and a mosaic floor depicting the Bramblewood — the town, the forest, the mushrooms, the guardian, all of it. Around the edge of the room are five stone pedestals, each with a symbol: a flame, a leaf, a drop of water, a paw print, and a heart.
The root bracelet from the Guardian glows when held near the heart pedestal. Placing it there causes that symbol to light up. The party needs to figure out the other four:

Flame — light a torch and hold it near (or a fire cantrip)
Leaf — place any natural item from the forest (the fox's fur works, or a leaf from the Sunny Edge if they grabbed one)
Water — pour water from the flooded room (if they thought to bring any) or a water cantrip
Paw print — the fox steps on it on its own, without being asked, right when the party is stuck

That last one should get a reaction. The fox knew.
The door opens with a sound like wind through branches and the smell of something green and alive rushing out.

🌿 The Final Chamber: The Sleeping Grove
Not what anyone expected. No dungeon. No bones. A vast underground cavern with a domed ceiling covered in carved stars, a floor of soft moss, and in the center — a enormous stone cradle covered in vines, with a figure sleeping inside.
She is called Mira of the Roots. Ancient forest spirit, half the reason the Bramblewood exists at all. Not evil. Not a monster. Something went wrong long ago — a curse, a sadness, a forgetting — and she fell into a sleep so deep the forest started dying around her without knowing why. The glowing mushrooms were her dreaming. The silence was her forgetting.
The Bramble Guardian made her. He misses her.
The Final Encounter is not a fight.
Waking Mira requires three things:

A kind word — someone must speak to her, tell her something true and good about the forest above
A brave act — someone must step forward alone into the dark center of the room where it's scariest and call her name clearly: Mira
Something given freely — not taken, not traded. Given. The party has to decide what to offer. A player's favorite item. Marta's muffin. Fuzz's spoon.

If Fuzz gives the spoon — and he will, if the party hesitates long enough, he will just quietly walk forward and place it on the cradle — that is the moment the campaign earns.
Mira wakes. The carved stars on the ceiling begin to glow. The moss blooms with tiny white flowers. She sits up slowly, looks at the party with enormous moss-green eyes, and says:
"Oh. You're very small. And very brave. Thank you for remembering me."
She rises, the catacombs fill with warm golden light, and the crack in the tree above bursts open with the sound of birdsong flooding back into the Bramblewood.

npcs.name: The Stranger
npcs.notes: Sits alone in the corner of The Drowsy Badger, hood up, nursing the same drink for hours. Pale, quiet, watches the door. If the players approach, they find a young person with hollow eyes and mud-caked boots from deep forest travel. They won't say where they came from, but their map — briefly visible before they fold it away — shows something drawn beneath the Bramblewood. Not threatening toward the party. Just scared. Could be an ally, could be a red herring, could be the key to the whole thing.

npcs.name: Old Wren the Druid
npcs.notes: Lives just outside town in a cottage covered in drying flowers. Ancient, possibly. Speaks in a slow, certain way that makes everything sound important. She knows the Bramblewood better than anyone and will tell the party: "The forest isn't angry. It's afraid. There's a difference." Very good at giving cryptic hints without being annoying about it.

npcs.name: Elara Stonebend the Blacksmith
npcs.notes: Tall, no-nonsense, deeply kind. She's been finding her tools moved around the shop at night and blames ghosts, which she says with total calm like it's a minor inconvenience. Will give the party a free piece of equipment if they promise to "sort out whatever's going on."

npcs.name: Pip
npcs.notes: age 7, local kid The unofficial town greeter. Pip will immediately try to befriend the players, show them his collection of "magic rocks" (they are regular rocks), and knows a secret path into the Bramblewood that he is absolutely not supposed to use. Very brave. Very small.

npcs.name: Marta Copperkettle, Tavernkeeper of The Drowsy Badger
npcs.notes: A stout, rosy-cheeked woman with flour perpetually in her hair (she also does the baking). Marta knows every piece of gossip in Willowmere and will trade information for compliments about her meat pies. She has a loud laugh, calls everyone "sweetheart," and secretly hasn't slept in two weeks because she keeps hearing humming from under the tavern floor.

monsters.notes: Short even for a goblin, wears a hat made from a stolen boot. Talks constantly, mostly about himself, mostly lies. Means well in a chaotic useless way.
Snip's best friend. Barely speaks. Has somehow accumulated seventeen spoons. Nobody knows where they come from. Not even Fuzz.

Luminara was not always ochre. Once she was pale and translucent like Vex and Drift, drifting through the underground spring that feeds the flooded chamber. But centuries of absorbing the starlit water — soaking in Mira's dreaming light — stained her slowly gold, then amber, then the deep warm ochre she is now. She is the oldest thing in the catacombs besides Mira herself and Cobweb, and she moves like she knows it.
She is enormous. Not aggressive. Just large and slow and completely unbothered by the party's presence in a way that is somehow more intimidating than if she were trying to eat them.
Why she's dangerous: Luminara doesn't sting like Vex and Drift. She engulfs. If someone stands still too long in the water near her she drifts over them like a warm blanket and begins dissolving whatever she touches. Not maliciously. She just doesn't always notice what's inside her. This is her whole thing.
Stats: Standard Ochre Jelly, CR 2. Resists all damage except slashing and fire. If hit with slashing damage she splits — the two smaller pieces glow dimmer and drift away confused. She cannot be permanently killed this way, just divided and disoriented. Moving quickly past her provokes an opportunity pseudopod attack: 2d6 acid.
The party avoids her entirely by moving slowly and staying near the walls. She occupies the center of the flooded room like a very large, very golden, very indifferent piece of furniture.

Mira is an Awakened Tree reskinned as a forest spirit — tall, willowy, made of living wood and flowering vines, moss-green eyes, voice like wind in leaves. She wakes disoriented and the first round after waking she makes a Wisdom saving throw DC 10 — on a failure she lashes out instinctively before recognizing the party as friends. This is the one moment combat could happen and it should feel like an accident not a fight. Regrow the Bramblewood to full health overnight
Her attack is Grasping Vines — 2d6 bludgeoning, restrained condition, but she releases immediately if anyone calls her name
Speak to any plant or animal
Grant the party the Blessing of the Root — advantage on all Nature and Animal Handling checks, lasts until the next long rest, and leaves a small flower blooming briefly wherever they step

Cobweb is a large spider — bigger than a cat, smaller than a dog — who has been living in the puzzle room for so long she has become part of the decor. Her webs are woven into the mosaic in genuinely beautiful patterns. She is not aggressive. She is territorial in the way a cat is territorial — she will puff up and posture if someone moves too fast near her corner.
She has eight eyes and all of them are watching.
Backstory: Cobweb hatched from an egg in this room before Mira fell asleep. She is the last creature Mira spoke to before the long sleep began. She doesn't understand what happened. She has been keeping the puzzle room clean and waiting for someone to come solve it for a very long time.
If the party is kind to her — offers food, speaks gently, doesn't scream — she climbs onto the heart pedestal and taps it with one leg, showing the party where the bracelet goes. She has been waiting to do this for decades.
Stats: Giant Spider, CR 1, but will not attack unless attacked first. If the party befriends her she follows them to the final chamber and sits on Mira's cradle when she wakes up, which is where she belongs.

dmNotes:
The Story
A forest spirit named Mira fell into a cursed sleep long ago and the forest she tended has been slowly going wrong ever since — silent, strange, creeping toward the edges of a small town called Willowmere. Nobody in town knows why things feel off. They just know the birds stopped singing and their children keep bringing home glowing mushrooms from the treeline.

The party arrives in Willowmere, figures out something is wrong, follows the clues into the Bramblewood, descends into the catacombs beneath an ancient oak, and wakes Mira up. The forest heals. Everyone goes home. Fuzz opens a spoon shop.

This is a campaign about kindness as a mechanic. Almost nothing is solved by combat. Almost everything is solved by paying attention, being gentle, and showing up for people and creatures who needed someone to notice them.


Act One — Willowmere
Goal: establish the town, meet the NPCs, find the hook

The Town
A drowsy little town at the edge of the Bramblewood forest where everyone knows everyone, the baker leaves muffins on your doorstep when you’re sick, and the worst thing that usually happens is old Mr. Thistlewick’s goat escaping again. Cobblestone paths wind between flower-box cottages, a small market square sits at the center with a splashing fountain, and paper lanterns hang between buildings that glow warm orange at night. Lately though, the forest has gone quiet — no birdsong, no rustling — and some of the children have started finding strange glowing mushrooms at the treeline.

Opening Scene
The party arrives in Willowmere. Describe the lanterns, the fountain, the flower boxes. Make it feel safe and warm and like exactly the kind of place worth saving. The hook develops naturally through the NPCs.

NPCs in Willowmere

Marta Copperkettle
Cleric | Tavernkeeper of The Drowsy Badger  |  AC 15 | HP 37 | Chaotic Neutral
A stout, rosy-cheeked woman with flour perpetually in her hair. Marta knows every piece of gossip in Willowmere and will trade information for compliments about her meat pies. She has a loud laugh, calls everyone “sweetheart,” and secretly hasn’t slept in two weeks because she keeps hearing humming from under the tavern floor.
Her muffins, if the party thinks to ask for provisions, matter later.

Pip
Bard | Age 7, Local Kid  |  AC 15 | HP 37 | Chaotic Neutral
The unofficial town greeter. Pip will immediately try to befriend the players, show them his collection of “magic rocks” (they are regular rocks), and knows a secret path into the Bramblewood he is absolutely not supposed to use. Very brave. Very small.
He is the party’s first guide and the reason they find the Sunny Edge at all.

Elara Stonebend
Fighter | Blacksmith  |  AC 15 | HP 37 | Chaotic Neutral
Tall, no-nonsense, deeply kind. She’s been finding her tools moved around the shop at night and blames ghosts, which she says with total calm like it’s a minor inconvenience. Will give the party a free piece of equipment if they promise to sort out whatever’s going on.

Old Wren
Druid | The Herb Woman  |  AC 15 | HP 37 | Chaotic Neutral
Lives just outside town in a cottage covered in drying flowers. Ancient, possibly. Speaks in a slow, certain way that makes everything sound important. She knows the Bramblewood better than anyone.
“The forest isn’t angry. It’s afraid. There’s a difference.”
She knows about the mushrooms and the humming. She will not go into the forest herself. She knows why.

The Stranger
Rogue | No name given yet  |  AC 15 | HP 37 | Chaotic Neutral | Secret: accidentally caused a past tragedy
Sits alone in the corner of The Drowsy Badger, hood up, nursing the same drink for hours. Pale, quiet, watches the door. Hollow eyes, mud-caked boots from deep forest travel. Their map — briefly visible before they fold it away — shows something drawn beneath the Bramblewood. Not threatening. Just scared.
If approached before leaving town, they give one warning: “Don’t go into the dark heart without something to give.” Then refuse to say more.
Their camp will be found in the Dark Heart. The note they leave at the end reads “I knew you could do it. — M” and what M stands for is the DM’s call.


Act Two — The Bramblewood
Goal: traverse the forest, collect the three key items, find the entrance

The Bramblewood starts friendly — tall oaks, soft moss, shafts of golden light. Ten minutes in, the light dims, the trees crowd together, and the path becomes a suggestion. Glowing mushrooms pulse faintly blue-green everywhere. They don’t hurt anyone. They’re just watching. (Probably.)

Zone One — The Sunny Edge
Fireflies even during the day. A small creek with smooth stones. A hollow log big enough to crawl through. Pip knows this part. The party finds their first real clue here: a child’s wooden toy, half-buried, that doesn’t belong to anyone in Willowmere.

Encounter: The Tangled Fox
A fox with its tail caught in enchanted brambles. Simple DEX check or good roleplay to free it. Once freed it follows the party and becomes important in Chamber Three of the catacombs. No stat block needed. Just a good fox.

Snip — Goblin   |   CR 1/4 | AC 15 | HP 7 (2d6)
Short even for a goblin. Wears a hat made from a stolen boot. Talks constantly, mostly about himself, mostly lies. Means well in a chaotic useless way. Found in the Sunny Edge with Fuzz, pretending to be on purpose here. They are not on purpose here.

Fuzz — Goblin   |   CR 1/4 | AC 15 | HP 7 (2d6)
Snip’s best friend. Barely speaks. Has somehow accumulated seventeen spoons. Nobody knows where they come from. Not even Fuzz. Special ability: Spoon Sense — once per session Fuzz produces a spoon that turns out to be exactly what the party needed. He will give his best spoon to Mira at the end if the party hesitates long enough. This is the emotional climax of the campaign. Don’t rush it.

Zone Two — The Mushroom Maze
Glowing mushrooms grow as tall as the players. The path splits constantly and looks identical every direction. Navigation is impossible by normal means.

The trick: hum or sing and the mushrooms pointing toward the center glow brighter. Old Wren would know this. Pip would accidentally discover it. Let the youngest player lead here. Her voice literally guides the party forward.

Encounter: The Spore Sprites
Three tiny fae, mischievous but not evil, redirecting travelers away from the center because they’re scared too. They speak in rhymes, steal small objects, and are won over with food — Marta’s muffins work perfectly. Once befriended they give the party a glowing seed that grows the bridge in Chamber Two. Use Pixie stat block, CR 1/4. They flee if combat starts.

Zone Three — The Dark Heart
Ancient enormous trees. Roots erupting from the ground like frozen waves. No mushrooms — that absence should feel wrong. No sound at all. A massive oak at the center with a crack down its trunk glowing faint red from far below.

The Stranger’s camp is here. Cold fire. Same boot prints. Scratched into the bark: “Don’t wake her until you’re ready.”

The Bramble Guardian — Treant (reskinned)   |   CR 5 | AC 13
A creature of twisted branches and old roots blocking the crack in the tree. Not a villain. It asks three questions:
Have you helped someone today?
Have you been afraid and done it anyway?
Do you promise to be careful down there?

Answer honestly — it steps aside and gives the party the root bracelet. Attack it — it wins, but always offers another chance to just answer the questions.

“She made me. I miss her. Bring her back if you can.”

Items Carried into the Catacombs

Item
Source
Used In
Glowing seed
Spore Sprites
Chamber Two — grows the root bridge
Root bracelet
Bramble Guardian
Chamber Three — heart pedestal
Marta’s muffins
Tavern
Spore Sprites or offered to Mira
Fox companion
Sunny Edge
Chamber Three — paw print pedestal



Act Three — The Catacombs
Goal: navigate three chambers, reach Mira, wake her

The crack in the ancient oak opens into a narrow stone staircase smelling of earth and old rain. The walls are carved — intentional, covered in flowing patterns of vines and stars and small animals. Someone built this a long time ago and built it beautifully. Torches in iron brackets burn with soft blue flame that never goes out. Whatever is down here has been expecting visitors.

Chamber One — The Hall of Echoes
A long corridor lined with alcoves, each containing a stone animal — a fox, a deer, a bird, an owl. Their eyes follow the party. Not threatening. Curious.

Talk to them — the torches brighten. Rush past ignoring them — a low rumble, torches dim. This chamber teaches the youngest player that kindness is the mechanic down here, not combat.

The five bats roost in the ceiling: Squeaky (loudest), Noctis (hangs in front of faces and stares), Thistle (slightly sticky fur, no one knows why). They trigger as a Swarm of Bats, CR 1/4, only if the party is loud and sudden. Disperses after one round automatically.

Chamber Two — The Flooded Room
Knee-deep perfectly still black water full of drifting stars — not reflection, actual tiny lights. Beautiful and eerie. The floor drops suddenly in the center. Too deep to cross.

Plant the glowing seed in a crack in the wall — it grows instantly into a root bridge arching across the deep section.

Luminara — Ochre Jelly   |   CR 2 | AC 8 | HP 45 (6d10+12)
Luminara was not always ochre. Centuries of absorbing the starlit water — soaking in Mira’s dreaming light — stained her slowly gold, then amber, then the deep warm ochre she is now. She is the oldest thing in the catacombs besides Mira and Cobweb, and she moves like she knows it. Enormous, slow, completely unbothered.

She doesn’t sting. She engulfs — she just doesn’t always notice what’s inside her. Avoid her by moving slowly and hugging the walls. If hit with slashing damage she splits into two dimmer confused pieces that drift apart. Moving quickly past her triggers a pseudopod opportunity attack: 2d6 acid.

Vex and Drift are also here — smaller jellyfish, ambient. Vex bumps into fast movers (1d4 lightning, purely reflexive). Drift is the size of a dinner plate and harmless. Move calmly and both part like a curtain.

Snip takes a shortcut and falls in the deep section. Fuzz pulls him out using a spoon as a reaching tool. This should not work. It does. This is their heroic moment.

Chamber Three — The Puzzle Room
Circular room. Locked stone door. Mosaic floor showing the entire Bramblewood. Five stone pedestals: flame, leaf, water, paw print, heart.

Cobweb — Giant Spider   |   CR 1 | AC 14 | HP 26 (4d10+4)
Large spider, bigger than a cat, smaller than a dog. Has lived here so long her webs are woven into the mosaic in genuinely beautiful patterns. Not aggressive. She hatched from an egg in this room before Mira fell asleep and is the last creature Mira spoke to before the long sleep. She has been keeping this room clean and waiting for someone to come solve it for a very long time.

Be kind to her — she climbs onto the heart pedestal and taps it with one leg. She follows the party to the final chamber and sits on Mira’s cradle when she wakes. That is where she belongs.

Puzzle Solutions:
Heart → root bracelet from the Guardian
Paw print → the fox steps on it on its own, right when the party is stuck. The fox knew.
Flame → torch or fire cantrip
Leaf → any natural item carried from the forest
Water → water carried from the flooded room, or water cantrip

The door opens with a sound like wind through branches and the smell of something green and living rushes out.

The Final Chamber — The Sleeping Grove
A vast underground cavern. Domed ceiling covered in carved stars. Floor of soft moss. Complete silence. In the center — a stone cradle covered in vines, with a figure sleeping inside.

Mira of the Roots — Forest Spirit   |   CR 2 | AC 13 | HP 50
Tall, willowy, made of living wood and flowering vines. Moss-green eyes. Voice like wind in leaves. Ancient. The reason the Bramblewood exists at all. She fell into a cursed sleep long ago and the forest has been dying slowly without understanding why. The glowing mushrooms were her dreaming. The silence was her forgetting.

Waking Mira requires three things — this is not a fight:
A kind word — speak to her, tell her something true and good about the forest above
A brave act — step forward alone into the darkest part of the room and call her name clearly: Mira
Something given freely — not traded, not taken. The party decides. Fuzz will give his spoon if they hesitate.

On waking: Wisdom save DC 10. On a failure she lashes out once in confusion — Grasping Vines, 2d6 bludgeoning, restrained. She releases immediately if anyone says her name. This is an accident, not an attack.

When fully awake:
Regrows the Bramblewood to full health overnight
Speaks to any plant or animal
Grants the party Blessing of the Root — advantage on Nature and Animal Handling checks until next long rest, tiny flowers bloom briefly wherever they step

“Oh. You’re very small. And very brave. Thank you for remembering me.”

Look at the youngest player when you say this line.


Resolution — Back in Willowmere
Run this as pure description — no mechanics, just moments. The forest is loud again by morning.

Marta slept. There are celebratory pies. Enough for Snip and Fuzz.
Pip meets Mira at the treeline and shows her his rocks. She tells him one of them actually is magic. He was right all along.
Elara’s tools are exactly where she left them.
Old Wren just nods.
The Stranger is gone. A note on their corner table: “I knew you could do it. — M”
Snip and Fuzz stay. Fuzz opens a spoon shop. It has one customer.


Pacing Notes for a Young Player
Keep sessions to 60–90 minutes maximum
Let her name things — the fox, the bats, Cobweb. Ownership builds investment
Make her the hero of at least one moment per session — the mushroom maze singing is built for this
Mira should look at her specifically when she wakes up. Direct that moment to the youngest player
Fuzz giving the spoon is the emotional climax — don’t rush past it, let it land
The note from the Stranger is the last thing they see — end on mystery so she wants to come back
